singleBlock.title: Single Block Basics
singleBlock.p1: A block contains data, a nonce (number used once), and produces a hash.
singleBlock.p2: The hash is calculated from the block number, nonce, and data using SHA-256.
singleBlock.li1: Click "Mine" to find a nonce that produces a hash starting with 4 zeros.
singleBlock.li2: Try changing the data after mining - notice how the hash changes and the block becomes invalid (background turns red).
singleBlock.p3: This demonstrates why blockchain data is immutable - any change is immediately visible.

blockchain.title: Blockchain Fundamentals
blockchain.p1: Block #1 is the "Genesis Block" - the first block in the chain with no previous hash.
blockchain.p2: Each subsequent block includes the hash of the previous block, linking them together.
blockchain.li1: Mine each block to create a valid chain (all blocks turn green).
blockchain.li2: Try changing data in an earlier block - all subsequent blocks become invalid!
blockchain.p3: This chain reaction shows why blockchains are tamper-evident: changing one block breaks the entire chain.
blockchain.p4: To fix the chain, you must re-mine the altered block and all blocks after it.

digitalSignature.p1: Digital signatures prove that a message came from a specific person.
digitalSignature.li1: A private key is kept secret and used to sign messages.
digitalSignature.li2: A public key can be shared with everyone and used to verify signatures.
digitalSignature.p2: The signature changes if the message is altered, making tampering detectable.
digitalSignature.p3: In blockchains, signatures prove who authorized a transaction without revealing their private key.

transaction.title: Transaction & Signatures
transaction.p1: A transaction contains sender (from), receiver (to), and amount.
transaction.p2: The transaction data is hashed and signed using the sender's private key.
transaction.li1: If any transaction data changes, the signature becomes invalid.
transaction.li2: Verification uses the sender's public key.
transaction.p3: This ensures only the owner of the private key can authorize a transaction.

blockchainPage.title: Blockchain
blockchainPage.description: Create an interconnected sequence of blocks. Changing data in an earlier block breaks the chain!
